title: South Africa Financial Update | Key Market & Economic Developments | 23 July 2026
summary: SARB holds the repo rate at 7.00% in a 4-2 vote as 2026 GDP growth is forecast at 1.2%, while ARM unveils a R16bn capex plan, Pepkor eyes a R21.3bn fintech spin-off, and Mozambique's looming 2028 gas cliff threatens industrial energy costs.
body:
The South African Reserve Bank (SARB) has kept the repo rate unchanged at 7.00%, leaving the prime lending rate at 10.50%. The Monetary Policy Committee's decision was split 4–2, with four members opting to hold rates steady while two supported a 25-basis-point increase. Although headline inflation edged up to 5.0% in June, driven by fuel price volatility and persistent services inflation, easing global oil prices and a resilient rand provided enough support for the Bank to pause further tightening. Economic growth expectations remain modest, with GDP forecast at around 1.2% for 2026, reflecting high operating costs and subdued consumer and business confidence.

On the corporate front, African Rainbow Minerals (ARM) came under pressure after unveiling a nearly R16 billion capital investment plan focused on expanding its mining operations. Pepkor announced a significant R21.3 billion fintech expansion strategy, with long-term ambitions of listing the business separately as it strengthens its presence in South Africa's informal economy. Meanwhile, Absa is nearing a strategic transaction in Tanzania that would consolidate approximately R49.2 billion in regional banking assets. In retail, Mr Price reported encouraging quarterly sales growth, supported by continued strong demand across its NKD business.

Attention is also turning to longer-term structural challenges. Industry stakeholders continue to warn about South Africa's looming 2028 gas cliff, as natural gas supplies from Mozambique's Pande and Temane fields are expected to decline significantly after 2028. Without accelerated investment in LNG infrastructure, many industrial users could face energy costs between two and five times higher if forced to switch to LPG or diesel alternatives. At the same time, the technology sector is responding to a recent cyberattack that prompted Mustek and Rectron to launch forensic recovery efforts, while Treasury continues to defend funding freezes affecting 69 financially distressed municipalities, highlighting ongoing concerns over municipal finances and their impact on the broader business environment.
author: Business News Hub Desk

title: South Africa Financial Update | Key Market & Economic Developments | 24 July 2026
summary: SARB surprises markets by holding the repo rate at 7.00% in a 4-2 vote despite hotter June inflation, lifts 2026 GDP forecast to 1.4%, while transport inflation spikes 12.7% and municipalities weigh solar grid-access fees.
body:
The South African Reserve Bank (SARB) surprised markets by keeping the repo rate unchanged at 7.00%, leaving the prime lending rate at 10.50%. Governor Lesetja Kganyago said the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) voted 4–2 in favour of holding rates steady. Most economists had expected a 25-basis-point increase after June's higher-than-anticipated inflation reading.

The decision reflects a balancing act between rising inflation risks and a still-fragile economy. While higher global oil prices, fuelled by supply disruptions in the Middle East, have pushed transport costs higher, broader consumer and services inflation has remained relatively contained. The SARB also lifted its 2026 economic growth forecast to 1.4%, up from 1.2%, although it cautioned that economic activity is likely to remain subdued during the second and third quarters as consumer and business confidence stays under pressure.

June's headline consumer inflation accelerated to 5.0% year-on-year, largely driven by a 12.7% increase in transport inflation following earlier fuel price hikes. Looking ahead, preliminary figures from the Central Energy Fund (CEF) suggest motorists could see a modest petrol price cut of around 50 cents per litre in August. Diesel users, however, may face increases of between 60 and 90 cents per litre as global supply constraints continue to influence oil markets. Elevated energy prices and ongoing supply chain disruptions remain the biggest inflation risks for the months ahead.

In the business sector, Business Leadership South Africa (BLSA) has warned that the pace of structural reform is beginning to slow. The organisation stressed that improvements to transport infrastructure and local government performance are essential if South Africa is to achieve stronger and more sustainable economic growth.

Meanwhile, a growing number of financially distressed municipalities are considering introducing fixed grid-access charges for households with solar installations. The move is aimed at recovering declining electricity revenue as more consumers reduce their dependence on the national grid.

Financial markets remain relatively stable, with the rand trading in the region of R16.50 to R16.60 against the US dollar. Despite persistent inflationary pressures, the SARB's decision to leave interest rates unchanged signals confidence that inflation can be managed while continuing to support a modest economic recovery.
author: Business News Hub Desk

title: South African Business & Financial Update | 24 July 2026
summary: SARB weighs mandatory PayShap adoption for banks as instant payments lag global peers, its rate hold draws praise from property and agriculture sectors, Home Affairs launches Phase II of the Trusted Employer Scheme for fintech talent, and the dtic wraps its Essential Oils Conference in Pretoria.
body:
South Africa's economic landscape continues to evolve as policymakers, businesses, and industry leaders focus on reforms aimed at supporting long-term growth and improving competitiveness.

The South African Reserve Bank (SARB) is considering making participation in the PayShap fast-payment system mandatory for commercial banks. Three years after its launch, PayShap processes around 60 million transactions per month—equivalent to roughly one transaction per person each month, well below adoption levels seen in countries such as Brazil. According to SARB executive Pradeep Maharaj, wider use of instant digital payments could reduce reliance on cash and potentially add up to one percentage point to South Africa's GDP growth over time.

The Reserve Bank's decision to keep the repo rate at 7.00% and the prime lending rate at 10.50% has been broadly welcomed across several sectors. Property market analysts from Standard Bank Home Services and the ooba Group said stable interest rates provide much-needed certainty for homebuyers by keeping monthly repayments predictable, particularly in the first-time buyer and affordable housing markets. The agricultural sector also welcomed the pause in borrowing costs, although farmers continue to face pressure from higher diesel and fertiliser prices linked to ongoing global supply constraints. Economists at PSG Financial Services noted that the Monetary Policy Committee adopted a more measured tone than many had anticipated, choosing to monitor Middle East energy risks rather than immediately factoring them into its central economic outlook.

Meanwhile, the Department of Home Affairs has officially launched Phase II of the expanded Trusted Employer Scheme (TES). The latest phase introduces dedicated visa pathways designed to attract international financial, fintech, and technical professionals to South Africa's emerging Synthetic Financial Centre (SFC). The initiative is expected to significantly reduce visa processing times, helping strengthen the country's competitiveness as a destination for highly skilled talent.

On the trade front, the Department of Trade, Industry and Competition (the dtic) is wrapping up its Essential Oils Conference in Pretoria, held from 23 to 24 July. The conference forms part of a broader strategy to help local producers move beyond exporting raw natural resources and expand into higher-value manufacturing for the global cosmetics, wellness, and essential oils market, which is valued at more than US$15 billion.
author: Business News Hub Desk

title: South African Corporate & Market Update | 24 July 2026
summary: Deputy Finance Minister David Masondo resigns as PIC board chair amid governance tensions, Pepkor unveils a R21.3bn fintech spin-off plan, ARM commits R16bn to mine modernisation and solar capacity, industry warns on the 2028 gas cliff, and Mustek/Rectron continue cyberattack recovery.
body:
Several major corporate and institutional developments are shaping South Africa's business landscape, with leadership changes, strategic investments, and infrastructure challenges remaining firmly in focus.

Deputy Finance Minister David Masondo has officially stepped down as Chairperson of the Public Investment Corporation (PIC) board. His resignation follows weeks of reported governance tensions at the state-owned asset manager, which oversees more than R2.6 trillion in assets on behalf of public sector pension funds. Masondo said the decision was taken in the interests of stability and public confidence, underscoring the importance of strong leadership at Africa's largest asset manager for both investors and the broader financial markets.

Retail group Pepkor has announced a major restructuring that will establish a dedicated R21.3 billion fintech business, with long-term plans that could include a separate stock market listing. The strategy reflects Pepkor's continued expansion beyond discount retail into digital financial services, including micro-lending, digital payments, and insurance products aimed at South Africa's informal and lower-income markets.

Mining company African Rainbow Minerals (ARM) also drew investor attention after outlining an ambitious R16 billion capital investment programme. The planned expenditure will focus on modernising deep-level mining operations, expanding renewable energy capacity through solar generation, and strengthening logistics to reduce the impact of ongoing rail infrastructure constraints.

Energy experts and industry groups have renewed warnings about South Africa's looming "gas cliff", expected around 2028, as natural gas supplies from Sasol's mature Mozambican fields begin to decline. The anticipated shortfall could place significant pressure on manufacturing hubs in Mpumalanga and KwaZulu-Natal, increasing the urgency for government and the private sector to accelerate investment in liquefied natural gas (LNG) import infrastructure and alternative energy solutions.

Meanwhile, JSE-listed ICT distributor Mustek and its subsidiary Rectron are continuing recovery efforts following a significant cyberattack that disrupted parts of their internal operational networks. Both companies have launched forensic investigations to determine the cause and extent of the incident while working to restore affected systems and strengthen cybersecurity measures.
author: Business News Hub Desk

title: South African Infrastructure, Trade & Market Update | 24 July 2026
summary: Durban's DCT Pier 2 posts an 18% improvement in vessel turnaround as freight rail bottlenecks persist, Valterra Platinum forecasts earnings up more than 1,300% on a PGM price surge, AGOA renewal talks intensify for SA exporters, and SARB models a $100/barrel oil scenario that could delay its inflation target to mid-2027.
body:
South Africa's economic outlook continues to be shaped by improvements in logistics, stronger commodity markets, evolving trade discussions, and ongoing global energy risks.

Transnet's efforts to introduce greater private-sector participation are beginning to show encouraging results at the Durban Container Terminal (DCT) Pier 2. Average vessel turnaround times have improved by approximately 18% month-on-month, providing early signs that congestion at one of the country's busiest ports is easing. While progress at the ports is welcome, freight rail remains a significant challenge. Bulk export corridors, particularly those transporting coal and iron ore, continue to experience bottlenecks that are limiting mining exports and weighing on industry revenues.

South Africa's platinum group metal (PGM) producers are benefiting from a sharp recovery in global commodity prices. Valterra Platinum has issued a strong trading update, forecasting headline earnings to increase by more than 1,300%, to between R18.5 billion and R22.2 billion. The turnaround has been driven by an 85% rise in the US dollar PGM basket price, combined with improved operational performance following maintenance disruptions experienced during 2025.

Trade officials are also closely monitoring discussions in Washington over the future of the African Growth and Opportunity Act (AGOA). South Africa's automotive, agricultural, and wine industries rely heavily on the agreement's duty-free access to the US market. With the current framework approaching its renewal deadline, government trade representatives are advocating for an early extension to provide certainty for exporters and avoid disruptions to manufacturing and export planning for 2027.

Following its decision to leave interest rates unchanged, the South African Reserve Bank (SARB) also outlined the economic scenarios underpinning its latest forecasts. The Bank's baseline outlook assumes oil prices remain close to current levels, allowing inflation to gradually return towards its 3% target over the medium term. However, it also modelled a more adverse scenario in which crude oil prices rise to US$100 per barrel throughout 2026 as a result of escalating tensions in the Middle East. In that case, higher fuel costs could keep inflation above the Reserve Bank's target until at least mid-2027, potentially requiring further interest rate increases to contain price pressures.
author: Business News Hub Desk
